a cold $11, 900, 900 in his attempt to

Hutch and Ed Partridge made and
lost fortunes.


Never in the history of the grain
trade has there been a deal so long
drawn out or of such magnitude as
the recent May wheat deal.


Never have the principals on the
bull and the bear sides been able to
cover their tracks and hide their
identity as in this monster deal.


Never have the fluctuations been
wider and never was the outlook as
good for the bulls as at odd times
since last September--the time of the
inauguration of the bull deal.


Who the Bulls Were.


John w. Gates of New York, finan-
cial king.


Charles M. Schwab, the steel king
Corwin II. Spencer. multi-million-
aire grain man of st. Louis.


A. d. Thompson, multimillionaire
grain man of Duluth.



bury Washburh milling concern of
Minneapolis


Robert Pringle of Pringle, Fitch &
Rankin. big board of trade house.
John Sickel of Slckel. Roberts &
Co., board of trade house.


The Bears Were:


ArmourV Valentine. elevator and
grain men, with millions at their call
Norman b. Ream, financier and
large grain and stock speculator
Peavey Elevator company, with
houses in all sections of the country.
John Milliken of st. Louis, cash
grain and elevator man.


Harris & Scotch, grain and ele-
vator men and former partners in
the house of Harris, Gates & Co.


Both of the Rosenbaum concerns
large cash grain handlers and ele-
vator men.


Van Decentralization, elevator
men of the northwest.


Big Money on Either Side.


While Gates and his Wall street
bull clique have millions of dollars at
their backs, the bears have all the
money necessary to finance IA deal of
great magnitude.


The recent fight was n battle of
millionaires and 1t was to a finish.
